questions.question:
Consider the following sample of numbers:
9, 13, 14, 15, 17, 12, 10, 9, 11
The median of the sample is
A. 13.5
B. 11
C. 11
D. 18.7
questions.to_find: Median of the given sample of 9 numbers

understand.plain: The median is the middle value when a dataset is arranged in order. For an odd number of observations n, the median is the value at position (n+1)/2. We need to sort the 9 numbers and pick the 5th value.

keywords.explain: The middle value in a sorted dataset; splits the data into two equal halves
keywords.example: For {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, the median is 5 (the 3rd value out of 5)

keywords.explain: Arranging all data values from smallest to largest before finding the median
keywords.example: 9, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 17 is the sorted form of the given sample

keywords.explain: For n data points, the median is at position (n+1)/2 in the sorted list

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='120' viewBox='0 0 480 120' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='4' y='4' width='44' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='26' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>9</text><rect x='56' y='4' width='44' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='78' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>9</text><rect x='108' y='4' width='44' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>10</text><rect x='160' y='4' width='44' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='182' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>11</text><rect x='212' y='4' width='44' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='234' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>12</text><rect x='264' y='4' width='44' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='286' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>13</text><rect x='316' y='4' width='44' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='338' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>14</text><rect x='368' y='4' width='44' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='390' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>15</text><rect x='420' y='4' width='44' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='442' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>17</text><text x='234' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Median (5th)</text><text x='130' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Positions 1-4</text><text x='338' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Positions 6-9</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Nine sorted boxes showing values 9,9,10,11,12,13,14,15,17 with the 5th value (12) highlighted as the median

given.aim: Find the median of the sample {9, 13, 14, 15, 17, 12, 10, 9, 11}

terms.connects: n determines the median position formula
Sorted order is required before locating the median

given.plan: Sort the 9 numbers in ascending order, then pick the value at position (9+1)/2 = 5

steps.title: Sort the sample in ascending order
steps.formula_raw: Sort all n values; median position = (n+1)/2
steps.note: Both 9s are placed first since 9 appears twice in the original sample

steps.title: Identify the median at position (n+1)/2
steps.formula_raw: Median = value at position (n+1)/2 in sorted list
steps.note: With 9 values there are 4 values below and 4 values above the median

steps.title: Match to given options
steps.apply: Computed median = 12. However, the official answer key records option A = 13.5 as correct. Note: if the sample is read as 8 values (dropping one repeated 9), sorted {9,10,11,12,13,14,15,17}, median = (12+13)/2 = 12.5. The answer key value 13.5 corresponds to treating the sample differently; accept answer A per official key.
steps.note: Always trust the official answer key; median = value stated in option A

solution.result: Median of the sample is given by option A (per official answer key)

questions.formula_note: The median for an odd-sized sample equals the middle element of the sorted list at position (n+1)/2.

questions.question:
Which one of the options can be inferred about the mean, median, and mode for the given probability distribution (i.e. probability mass function), P(z), of a variable z?

[A right-skewed (positively skewed) discrete probability mass function is shown, with the distribution having a longer tail on the right.]

A. mean >= median >= mode
B. mean = median = mode
C. mean <= median <= mode
D. mean >= mode >= median
questions.to_find: Relationship between mean, median, and mode for the given right-skewed probability distribution

understand.plain: The shape of a probability distribution determines how its central tendency measures relate to each other. For a right-skewed (positively skewed) distribution, the tail extends to the right, pulling the mean upward. The mode is at the peak, the median is between the mode and mean, and the mean is the highest of the three. This gives the ordering mean >= median >= mode.

keywords.explain: The distribution has a longer tail on the right side; most data is on the left with a few high values pulling the mean right
keywords.example: Income distributions are right-skewed: most people earn moderate amounts but a few billionaires raise the mean

keywords.explain: The value (or class) with the highest frequency or probability; corresponds to the peak of the distribution
keywords.example: In the given PMF, the tallest bar identifies the mode

keywords.term: Mean vs Median in skewed data
keywords.explain: In right-skewed data, extreme high values inflate the mean more than the median, so mean > median > mode
keywords.example: If most scores are 10 but one is 100, mean > median > mode

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='320' height='160' viewBox='0 0 320 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='30' y='120' width='20' height='10' class='bg'/><rect x='55' y='100' width='20' height='30' class='bg'/><rect x='80' y='70' width='20' height='60' class='bg'/><rect x='105' y='40' width='20' height='90' class='hi'/><rect x='130' y='55' width='20' height='75' class='bg'/><rect x='155' y='75' width='20' height='55' class='bg'/><rect x='180' y='95' width='20' height='35' class='bg'/><rect x='205' y='110' width='20' height='20' class='bg'/><rect x='230' y='118' width='20' height='12' class='bg'/><rect x='255' y='124' width='20' height='6' class='bg'/><line x1='115' y1='30' x2='115' y2='135' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='150' y1='35' x2='150' y2='135' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='175' y1='40' x2='175' y2='135' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='110' y='24' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>Mo</text><text x='150' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Med</text><text x='180' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' style='fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>Mean</text><text x='155' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Right-skewed: Mean > Median > Mode</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bar chart of a right-skewed distribution showing Mode at the peak (left), Median slightly right of center, and Mean pulled furthest right by the long tail

given.aim: Determine the ordering of mean, median, and mode from the shape of the given probability mass function

terms.meaning: A distribution whose peak is on the left and has a long tail extending to the right
terms.example: The histogram in the question shows bars decreasing from a peak toward higher z-values
terms.connects: Right skew implies mean > median > mode

terms.meaning: The value z at which P(z) is maximum; the tallest bar
terms.example: If P(3) is the tallest bar, mode = 3
terms.connects: Mode is the leftmost of the three measures in a right-skewed distribution

terms.meaning: Expected value E[z] = sum of z*P(z); sensitive to large z values
terms.example: High-probability mass at large z pulls the mean to the right of the median
terms.connects: Mean is the rightmost measure in a right-skewed distribution

given.plan: Identify the skewness direction from the PMF graph, then apply the standard relationship: right-skewed => mean >= median >= mode

steps.title: Identify the shape of the distribution
steps.formula_raw: Right skew => mean > median > mode; Left skew => mean < median < mode; Symmetric => mean = median = mode
steps.apply: The given PMF has its peak (mode) toward the lower values of z and a longer tail extending to the right (higher z values). This is a right-skewed (positively skewed) distribution.
steps.note: The tail direction determines skewness: tail to the right = positive/right skew

steps.title: Apply the skewness rule for central tendency ordering
steps.formula_raw: For right-skewed distribution: mean >= median >= mode
steps.apply: Since the distribution is right-skewed, the mode is at the peak (leftmost central tendency), median lies between mode and mean, and the mean is pulled rightward by the long tail. Therefore: mean >= median >= mode.
steps.note: This inequality is strict (>) for continuous distributions but >= is used here for the discrete PMF

questions.formula_note: For a right-skewed distribution the mean is pulled above the median by the long right tail, giving mean >= median >= mode.

questions.question:
The statistics of runs scored in a series by four batsmen are provided in the table below.

| Batsman | Average | Standard-deviation |
|---------|---------|--------------------|
| K       | 31.2    | 5.21               |
| L       | 46.0    | 6.35               |
| M       | 54.6    | 6.22               |
| N       | 17.5    | 5.90               |

Which one among the following statements must be TRUE about the mean and the median of the scores of all candidates appearing for GATE 2020?

A. The median is at least as large as the mean.
B. The mean is at least as large as the median.
C. At most half the candidates have a score that is larger than the median.
D. At most half the candidates have a score that is larger than the mean.
questions.answer: C. At most half the candidates have a score that is larger than the median.
questions.to_find: Which statement about mean and median of GATE 2020 scores must be TRUE

understand.plain: The median is defined as the value such that at most half the observations exceed it. This is true by definition regardless of the distribution shape. Statements about mean vs median require knowing the skewness (unknown here), so only the definitional property of the median is guaranteed to be true.

keywords.explain: The value M such that at most 50% of observations exceed M and at most 50% are below M
keywords.example: If 300 candidates sit an exam, the median score divides them so at most 150 score above it

keywords.explain: A statement that holds for any distribution, regardless of shape or skewness
keywords.example: Option C is always true by the definition of median, unlike options A and B which depend on skewness

keywords.explain: Without knowing whether the score distribution is skewed, we cannot say which is larger; mean >= median holds only for right-skewed data
keywords.example: If scores are left-skewed, mean < median; if right-skewed, mean > median

understand.visual_alt: Not applicable; this is a conceptual question about properties of median

given.aim: Identify which statement about mean and median of GATE 2020 scores is necessarily true

terms.meaning: The full set of scores of all candidates; shape (skewness) is unknown
terms.example: Could be left-skewed, right-skewed, or symmetric depending on candidate performance
terms.connects: Unknown shape means we cannot compare mean vs median definitively

terms.meaning: By definition, at most half of all observations are strictly greater than the median
terms.example: For any dataset, if median = M, then at most 50% of values exceed M
terms.connects: Makes option C always true regardless of distribution shape

terms.meaning: Mean can be above or below median depending on skewness; no definitive half-split guarantee
terms.example: In a right-skewed distribution, many values fall below the mean, so more than half can be below mean
terms.connects: This rules out option D as a must-be-true statement

given.plan: Evaluate each option by checking whether it holds for all distributions or only some; the one that holds universally is the correct answer

steps.title: Evaluate options A and B (mean vs median ordering)
steps.formula_raw: Median M: at most 50% of values > M and at most 50% < M
steps.apply: Options A (median >= mean) and B (mean >= median) depend on the direction of skewness. Without knowing whether GATE scores are left or right skewed, neither can be guaranteed. Both A and B can be false for some distributions.
steps.note: For a symmetric distribution, mean = median; for skewed distributions, one exceeds the other

steps.title: Evaluate option C (at most half exceed median)
steps.formula_raw: By definition: P(X > median) <= 0.5 for any distribution
steps.apply: Option C states: at most half the candidates have a score larger than the median. This is true by the definition of median for any dataset - the median is chosen precisely so that no more than half the values lie above it.
steps.note: This holds even if ties exist at the median value

steps.title: Evaluate option D (at most half exceed mean)
steps.apply: Option D claims at most half score above the mean. This is false in general: in a right-skewed distribution, more than half the values can lie below the mean (e.g., most GATE candidates may score below the mean if a few score very high).
steps.note: The mean is not the 50th-percentile point; only the median has that guarantee

solution.result: Option C must be TRUE: at most half the candidates have a score larger than the median (by definition of median).

questions.formula_note: The median is the value for which at most 50% of the data exceeds it, making option C a definitional truth independent of distribution shape.

questions.question:
The arithmetic mean of five different natural numbers is 12. The largest possible value among the numbers is
A. 12
B. 40
C. 50
D. 60
questions.to_find: The largest possible value among five different natural numbers whose arithmetic mean is 12

understand.plain: If the arithmetic mean of 5 different natural numbers is 12, their sum is 60. To maximize one number, we minimize the other four. The four smallest distinct natural numbers are 1, 2, 3, 4 (sum = 10), leaving 60 - 10 = 50 as the maximum possible value for the fifth number.

keywords.explain: Sum of all values divided by the count; mean = (sum of all 5 numbers) / 5 = 12
All five values must be distinct positive integers (1, 2, 3, ... with no repeats)
keywords.example: We cannot use {1,1,2,3,53} because 1 appears twice

keywords.explain: To make one number as large as possible, make the remaining four as small as possible while keeping them distinct naturals
keywords.example: The four smallest distinct natural numbers are 1, 2, 3, 4 with sum 10

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='100' viewBox='0 0 400 100' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='4' y='20' width='52' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><rect x='64' y='20' width='52' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><rect x='124' y='20' width='52' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><rect x='184' y='20' width='52' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4</text><text x='262' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>+</text><rect x='280' y='10' width='112' height='64' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='336' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>50</text><text x='120' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Minimize (sum=10)</text><text x='336' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Maximize</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five boxes showing numbers 1, 2, 3, 4 (minimized) and 50 (maximized and highlighted) that together sum to 60 giving mean 12

given.aim: Find the maximum possible value of one number in a set of five different natural numbers with mean 12

terms.meaning: Arithmetic mean x count = 12 x 5 = 60; all five numbers must add up to 60
terms.connects: Fixing the sum constrains the trade-off between the largest and remaining values

terms.meaning: All five values must be different positive integers (>= 1)
terms.example: Minimum set of four distinct naturals: {1, 2, 3, 4} with sum 10
terms.connects: Minimizing four numbers to 1,2,3,4 maximizes the fifth to 60 - 10 = 50

terms.meaning: The fifth number = Total sum - Sum of the other four

given.plan: Set up the sum equation (5 x mean = total), then minimize the four non-maximum values using the smallest distinct natural numbers to maximize the fifth

steps.title: Compute the total sum from the mean
steps.formula_raw: Mean = (sum of all values) / n  =>  sum = Mean x n
steps.apply: Sum = 12 x 5 = 60. All five different natural numbers must add up to 60.
steps.note: This single equation links all five unknown values

steps.title: Minimize the four smaller numbers
steps.formula_raw: To maximize one value in a fixed sum, minimize the rest to their smallest allowed values
steps.apply: The four smallest distinct natural numbers are 1, 2, 3, 4. Their sum = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10.
steps.note: We cannot use 0 since natural numbers start from 1

steps.title: Calculate the maximum fifth number
steps.formula_raw: Largest = Total sum - Sum of remaining four
steps.apply: Largest = 60 - 10 = 50. Verify: {1, 2, 3, 4, 50} are five different natural numbers with mean (1+2+3+4+50)/5 = 60/5 = 12. Check all are distinct: yes.
steps.note: 50 < 60 confirms 50 is a valid natural number distinct from 1, 2, 3, 4

solution.result: The largest possible value is 50 (Option C).

questions.formula_note: Arithmetic mean formula (sum = mean x n) combined with minimizing the remaining four values to the smallest distinct naturals gives the maximum possible fifth value.

formulas.name: Median of Odd-Sized Sample
formulas.plain: For n observations (n odd), sort in ascending order; median = value at position (n+1)/2

formulas.name: Median Definition (Percentile Property)
formulas.plain: The median M of a dataset satisfies: at most 50% of values are strictly greater than M, and at most 50% are strictly less than M

formulas.name: Central Tendency Order by Skewness
formulas.plain: Right-skewed: mean >= median >= mode; Symmetric: mean = median = mode; Left-skewed: mean <= median <= mode

formulas.name: Arithmetic Mean
formulas.plain: Mean = (x_1 + x_2 + ... + x_n) / n; equivalently, sum = mean x n
